name: Scales of Dike
description: The Scales of Dike are a divine artifact representing justice, balance, and moral order in Greek mythology. Forged by the primordial goddess Dike herself, the embodiment of justice, these scales were used to weigh the souls of the deceased in the Underworld, ensuring that each individual received their due reward or punishment based on the deeds performed during their lifetime. The scales are said to be incredibly sensitive, able to detect the slightest imbalance between good and evil. They are often described as being made of adamant, an unbreakable substance reserved for the gods, and imbued with Dike's own power to discern truth from falsehood. Some accounts suggest that the scales are constantly adjusting, reflecting the ever-changing state of moral balance in the world. The Scales of Dike are not merely a tool for judgment but a symbol of the cosmic order, a reminder that every action has consequences and that justice will ultimately prevail. Their presence ensures fairness and accountability, even in the face of chaos and injustice. The unwavering balance of the scales reflects Dike's commitment to upholding the natural law and maintaining harmony in the universe. Failure to uphold Dike's laws would cause the scales to tip, bringing chaos to the world. Only the purest of heart could operate the scales without them tipping, and causing imbalance.
subtitle: Justice, Balance, and Moral Order

keyMyths.title: Weighing of Souls
keyMyths.description: The Scales of Dike are used in the Underworld to weigh the souls of the dead, determining their fate in the afterlife. A heavier weight of evil deeds would condemn the soul to punishment.

keyMyths.title: Dike's Role
keyMyths.description: Dike, as the goddess of justice, uses the scales to ensure that cosmic balance is maintained and that transgressions against natural law are punished. Her scales ensure fairness.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Early Greek didactic poem outlining moral principles and the importance of justice.
sources.text: Early Greek didactic poem outlining moral principles and the importance of justice.

sources.author: Plato
sources.description: Philosophical dialogue discussing justice and the afterlife.
sources.text: Philosophical dialogue discussing justice and the afterlife.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: mentions Dike and the concept of justice in relation to the gods and humans
